ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried broccoli with scallops; closest parent 'stir-fried food' (FOODON_03315763) used as category anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Broccoli, cooked) used as closest parent; no composite stir-fried broccoli-scallop dish code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for stir-fried broccoli with scallops composite dish.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and oyster sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared plate of stir-fried broccoli with scallops, approximately 300 g, typically served as a side dish for 2–3 persons in a Chinese meal setting.

nutrient_source.title: Broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (USDA SR Legacy 11091) combined with Scallops, mixed species, cooked, steamed (USDA SR Legacy 15177) and estimated sauce contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for stir-fried broccoli with scallops in special sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: broccoli cooked (FDC 11091) weighted at approximately 55% of dish, scallops steamed (FDC 15177) at approximately 30%, and sauce/oil contribution at approximately 15%. Weighted composite per-100g values calculated. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; broccoli GI reference and low-GI seafood composite analogy
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Broccoli has a well-established GI of approximately 10–15 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). Scallops and oyster-based sauces contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Composite dish GI estimated at 15 (low), dominated by broccoli's very low GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (15 × 5.2 × 3.0) / 100 = 2.3 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is estimated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and oyster sauce in the special sauce; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium sauce preparation to keep a 300 g serving below 600 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a very low glycemic load of approximately 2.3 per serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high protein from scallops, and broccoli's cardioprotective micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Scallops provide lean protein with low saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g), while broccoli contributes vitamin C, folate, and potassium; the primary cardiovascular concern is sodium from the sauce, which should be moderated in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Broccoli with Scallops in Special Sauce is a Chinese-style wok dish popular in Singapore's dim sum and Cantonese restaurant settings. Fresh broccoli florets are blanched briefly and then wok-tossed over high heat with tender scallops, coated in a glossy special sauce typically comprising oyster sauce, light soy sauce, garlic, ginger, sesame oil, and a cornstarch slurry that creates a silky glaze. The dish delivers a satisfying contrast of textures — crisp-tender broccoli and plump, slightly sweet scallops — with an umami-rich, mildly savoury flavour profile. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with approximately 7.8 g protein and 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it one of the more nutritionally balanced options in a Chinese shared-meal context. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), dominated by broccoli's negligible glycemic contribution, and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 2.3, suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the sauce base, which is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Broccoli contributes meaningful amounts of vitamin C, folate, potassium, and dietary fibre, while scallops provide lean protein, vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. This dish is dairy-free but contains shellfish and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is typically served as a shared side dish in a multi-course Chinese meal.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for stir-fried broccoli with scallops in special sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked broccoli and steamed scallops with a weighted composite calculation. GI was estimated by analogy from broccoli's established low GI in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy FDC 11091 (broccoli, cooked) and FDC 15177 (scallops, steamed) with an estimated sauce/oil contribution. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries for broccoli or scallops at meaningful levels and was not calculable from the composite. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g as neither broccoli nor scallops contain trans fats and the sauce ingredients are not expected to contribute trans fats (confirmed zero by component source review). The dish is served at Loong Dim Sum, North Canal Road, Singapore, at SGD 21.00. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant's certification status is not verified from an approved source. The 'special sauce' composition is proprietary to the restaurant; the sauce nutrient contribution is estimated from standard Cantonese oyster-sauce-based stir-fry sauce profiles. Sodium estimate of 420 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-style sauce usage and may be higher in practice. A follow-up with HPB Singapore Foodo or direct laboratory analysis would improve confidence to high.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed canonical English food name removing restaurant-specific branding ('Loong's Special Sauce') to ensure non-promotional canonical identity.
Added all mandatory multilingual names for Singaporean Chinese cuisine: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Estimated composite nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy approved sources with transparent weighting methodology.
Set GI to 15 (low) by analogy from broccoli's established GI in University of Sydney GI Database; documented estimation basis.
Selected defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 closest_parent codes; set SNOMED CT to null with gap note.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 in notes as not reported in source.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero from component source review.
Verified all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Ensured all ontology match_type values use only allowed strings: exact, closest_parent, none.
